You always use an instance of as the root HomeKit object.

The sample app defines a class for use as a singleton that holds the one and only home manager for the app: You create an accessory list collection view controller to present the list of connected accessories.

Because this root view controller never gets deallocated, it can safely assign itself as the protocol delegate: The manager tells its delegate when the list of homes changes, including the first time the home manager loads data from the HomeKit database during initialization.
You can extend the app to allow the user to select among all known homes instead of always choosing the primary home.

This is the primary characteristic, and you should give users quick access to it.
To be able to use HomeKit, you enable the HomeKit capability and include the key in your app’s file, as described in .

To be able to build an app that has the HomeKit capability, you must set a valid signing team in the target’s General pane before you build, so that Xcode can create a provisioning profile containing the HomeKit entitlement.
If you don’t have any real home automation accessories, or if you want to try an accessory type that you don’t have, use the HomeKit Accessory Simulator (HAS), as described in .
You can use a mix of simulated devices and real ones.
You always use an instance of as the root HomeKit object, following the pattern described in .
Use the delegate to set the home view’s property based on this information.
You can derive a name from this information, and use that to group accessories: Unlike the class, the class doesn’t natively contain the list of related accessories.

When the user taps an accessory in the home view, you use the method override to assign the corresponding accessory to the accessory view, which triggers the view to reload its content: When the user taps a service in the accessory view, you push a instance on the navigation stack.
For example, the garage door opener service might have a characteristic whose value you set to indicate whether the garage door should be opened or closed.
This particular implementation ensures that the home view always shows the primary home.
